chapter_title: When the Walls Began to Listen
text: By the time Elara reached Briar Hollow, dusk had already swallowed the road behind her. The house stood at the end of a dead lane, narrow and tall, with dark ivy clawing at its stone walls and every window reflecting a sky that had gone the color of ash. She had come to inventory the estate for a distant relative she had never met, and she told herself the cold in her chest came from the weather alone. Inside, the air smelled of cedar, dust, and something older, something faintly sweet that made her think of flowers left too long in a sealed room. Her footsteps echoed through halls lined with portraits whose eyes seemed slightly misaligned, as though each one had turned to watch her pass. In the study she found a ledger, a ring of iron keys, and a note written in trembling ink: If you hear your name, do not answer. Elara laughed at first, but the sound died quickly. From somewhere above came a soft knock. Then another. Then, clear as a whisper against her ear, a voice from the ceiling breathed, Elara. She froze. The house settled with a long wooden groan, and every door along the hallway slowly creaked open by itself. A draft moved past her, carrying the scent of damp earth from the cellar below. Against all reason, one portrait at the end of the corridor was different now. It showed not the stern woman she had noticed before, but an empty chair beside a fireplace, as if someone had stepped out of the painting only moments ago. The whisper came again, this time from behind the walls, slithering from room to room: Elara, come downstairs. Her lantern flickered hard enough to throw the shadows into wild motion. Then the front door slammed shut with a force that shook dust from the rafters, and from the darkness beneath the cellar door came the slow, deliberate sound of a key turning in a lock that no one had touched.
